articles.title: Dogecoin, Ethereum, Gold Trades On Your Mind? Here's What This Top Analyst Has Projected For The Coming Few Days
articles.description: A leading cryptocurrency analyst on Tuesday projected significant volatility for Dogecoin in the days ahead, while outlining a rosy outlook for gold. Which...

articles.title: DeFi yields are crashing so hard that they can't compete with a traditional savings account
articles.description: DeFi yields have collapsed below TradFi rates, forcing investors to face higher smart contract risks for lower returns as regulation and exploits mount.

articles.title: Regulation by hostility: the real legacy of Biden-era crypto policy
articles.description: Thorn argues that a recent New York Times op-ed rewrites history through omission, glossing over the collateral damage caused by the previous administration.

articles.title: OneCoin’s fallout lingers as US victims get a shot at recovery
articles.description: Investors in the cryptocurrency Ponzi scheme OneCoin may finally get some relief through a Department of Justice program, some 12 years after the project launched.

articles.title: Russia’s Sanctions-Busting Cryptocurrency Empire
articles.description: A Kremlin-backed fintech company is linked to massive trade in dual-use goods.

report.synthesis_report: The regulation of cryptocurrency is a contentious issue, with differing perspectives on the approach taken by the U.S. administration and the motivations behind certain cryptocurrency developments. Agent A highlights how Western sanctions have spurred the creation of "Western-proof" financial channels, citing the example of A7, a cryptocurrency regulated by Kyrgyz financial rules and backed by Promsvyazbank deposits, designed for sanctions evasion. This perspective also points to regulatory actions against fraudulent schemes like OneCoin, with authorities identifying and investigating it by 2015-2016 and the US Department of Justice making assets available to compensate victims. Agent B, conversely, argues that the Biden administration has "weaponized the banking system" against the cryptocurrency industry, leading to the "debanking" of lawful crypto businesses without due process. This viewpoint contends that the administration's approach lacks a reasoned regulatory framework and incorrectly dismisses the use of blockchain technology by major firms. The evidence suggests a complex landscape where regulatory actions, the pursuit of sanctions evasion, and concerns about due process and innovation all play significant roles in the ongoing debate surrounding cryptocurrency regulation. The visual analysis indicates a neutral framing and tone across the provided images, while the independent NLP validation shows a slightly negative average sentiment, with a mix of positive, negative, and neutral sentiments across individual articles.
loaded_language_removed: sanctions evasion at an industrial scale
key_agreements: Regulators are actively involved in identifying and investigating cryptocurrency-related activities.
Sanctions have influenced the development of alternative financial channels.
key_disagreements: The intent and impact of U.S. regulatory actions on the crypto industry (weaponization vs. necessary oversight).
The extent to which major financial institutions are utilizing blockchain technology.
The effectiveness and fairness of the current regulatory approach.

The Promsvyazbank backing ensures virtually unlimited liquidity.
It is also backed by Promsvyazbank’s deposits.
The Biden economists’ claim that no “giant tech firms” are using this technology is flatly wrong.

The extra return that once justified exposure to DeFi has largely disappeared, making it less attractive compared to traditional brokerages.

it was not a reasoned regulatory framework.

arguments: Western sanctions cut off targets from global finance, prompting the development of Western-proof financial channels, which the Kremlin supported through the creation of A7.
Regulators began to identify and investigate OneCoin by late 2015, with several national financial regulators adding it to their lists by 2016.

The US Department of Justice has made $40 million in assets available to compensate victims of the OneCoin Ponzi scam who purchased the cryptocurrency between 2014 and 2019 and experienced a net loss.
A7 offers A7A5, a cryptocurrency issued by Old Vector and regulated by Kyrgyz financial rules, backed by Promsvyazbank deposits, designed for sanctions evasion at an industrial scale.
The anonymity of cryptocurrency, where public ledgers record transfers between wallet addresses rather than identifiable individuals, facilitates sanctions evasion.
EU cryptocurrency regulation, MiCA, only applies to EU-based exchanges and cannot reach networks operating entirely outside European jurisdiction, such as A7/A7A5.
A7A5 turnover was estimated at $72 billion–$93 billion in 2025, equivalent to one-third of Russia’s imports bill, with A7 processing $39 billion in transactions linked to sanctions evasion.
The extra return that once justified exposure to DeFi has largely disappeared, making it less attractive compared to traditional brokerages.
evidence: Western sanctions cut off their targets from global finance, including the SWIFT messaging network, cross-border correspondent banking relationships, and clearing mechanisms for dollar payments.
For sanctioned economies, the workaround is obvious: developing Western-proof financial channels.
This is what the Kremlin set out to do in late 2024 , when it supported the creation of A7, a Moscow-based
Bulgaria's Financial Supervision Commission issued a warning about OneCoin, after which the company ceased all operations in the country.
By 2016, several other national financial regulators also had OneCoin on their lists.
By year’s end, Norway, Bulgaria, Finland, Sweden and Latvia were all investigating the project.
On April 13, the US Department of Justice said that $40 million in assets are available to anyone who purchased OneCoin between 2014 and 2019 and experienced a net loss.
This program marks a milestone for OneCoin victims, most of whom had no recourse to get back what they lost, until now
A7 offers access to a unique product: A7A5, a cryptocurrency issued by the obscure Kyrgyz firm Old Vector and regulated by Kyrgyz financial rules.
It is also backed by Promsvyazbank’s deposits.
The Promsvyazbank backing ensures virtually unlimited liquidity.
The ledger records transfers between wallet addresses, not identifiable individuals or firms—like a highway where every car is visible but none has a license plate identifying its owner.
MiCA, the EU’s cryptocurrency regulation, only applies to EU-based exchanges.
Therefore, the legislation cannot reach networks operating entirely outside European jurisdiction, such as A7/A7A5 or even tether.
Experts estimate that A7A5 turnover stood at around $72 billion–$93 billion in 2025, a range that is equivalent to as much as one-third of Russia’s entire imports bill.
Meanwhile, A7 processed some $39 billion in transactions linked to sanctions evasion, a figure roughly equivalent to Russia’s prewar annual import bill for high-tech—and often dual-use—goods.
For investors weighing up a 2%-3% yield on DeFi against 3.14% at a traditional brokerage, that context is hard to ignore.
The extra return that once justified the exposure has largely disappeared.
